lines: Welcome. Tonight is different from the other nights. We're not going to build an elaborate scene. We're not going to work through the body piece by piece in great detail. Tonight, we're going to do something that sounds simple and turns out to be one of the hardest things a human being can do.
But first, the body needs to arrive. Even stillness needs a foundation. So we start there.

Adjust your position. Move slowly. Find where your body wants to be tonight. Let your head settle into the pillow... let your arms fall wherever they fall... let your legs be heavy and slightly apart. If you need to shift your hips or roll your shoulders, do that now. Take your time. There's no one waiting for you to be ready.

Now notice the surface beneath you. The mattress. The sheets. The way the bed receives your full weight without complaint. It has been doing this every night for years, and it has never once failed at the job. You are supported. Physically, tangibly, right now. Supported.

Bring your awareness to your feet. Don't move them. Don't tense them. Just notice them. The temperature... the weight... whether they feel heavy or restless. The tops of the feet, the soles, the toes. Just visiting. Just acknowledging that they're there, at the far end of your body, done with the day.

Now your ankles. Your shins. Your calves. Feel the muscle and bone resting against the bed. Whether they're tight or loose, warm or cool... it doesn't matter. You're not trying to change anything. You're just moving your awareness through, the way you'd walk slowly through a house at night, touching each doorframe as you pass.

Your knees. Your thighs, the largest muscles in your body, pressing down into the mattress. Heavy with the accumulated effort of standing, walking, climbing stairs, shifting weight from foot to foot. All of that is done. Let the thighs rest in their full heaviness.

Your hips and your pelvis. The bowl of the body. The center of gravity. Feel the weight pooling here, settling deep, anchoring you to the bed. Your lower back, arched or flat, the spine doing whatever it does when you stop asking it to hold you upright. Your sacrum, that triangular bone at the base of the spine, pressing against the mattress. The foundation of the foundation.

Your belly. Soft. Not held in. Not braced. Just soft and round and breathing on its own. Your lower ribs expanding gently with each inhale, contracting with each exhale. Your chest, rising and falling. The sternum. The collarbones. The hollow of the throat.

Your shoulders. Let them drop now. They're almost certainly higher than you think. There's always another quarter inch they can fall. Let them fall it. Feel the distance between your ears and your shoulders increase. That space is freedom. That space is the day releasing its grip on you.

Your arms, from shoulders to elbows. The heavy bones of the upper arms. Your elbows, those knobby hinges. Your forearms, your wrists, the delicate machinery of your hands. Your fingers, curled or open, resting on the blanket or at your sides. Done gripping. Done typing. Done reaching. Done.

Your neck. The vertebrae stacked carefully, each one a small miracle of engineering. Let the muscles along the sides of your neck soften. The tendons at the back, releasing. Your throat, open and unguarded.

Your jaw. Let your teeth part. Let the hinge of the jaw loosen so your mouth falls open just slightly. Your tongue, resting loose at the bottom of your mouth. Your lips, soft. Your cheeks. The muscles around your eyes... these tiny muscles that work all day, focusing and squinting and expressing. Let them go slack. Your eyelids, heavy, resting closed. Your forehead, smoothing. The space between your eyebrows, wide open. The top of your head. The back of your skull against the pillow.

The whole body now. One continuous field of sensation, lying here, breathing. You've visited every part. You've given each region permission to stop. The body has been acknowledged. It can rest. And now we turn to something else.
Again. In... filling the lungs gently, not forcing... and out, letting the breath thin out at the end until it's barely there.

Once more. In... and out. And now let the breath go. Let it breathe you. You are no longer in charge of it. It continues on its own, quieter now, shallower, the breath of someone who is already nearly asleep.
"Be still, and know that I am God." Psalm forty-six, verse ten.

This verse comes from a psalm about chaos. The nations rage. The kingdoms totter. The earth melts. It is not a psalm about a peaceful afternoon. It is a psalm about a world falling apart, and in the middle of the noise and the destruction, a voice says... be still. Stop. The command is given not to the chaos but to the listener. You cannot stop the world from shaking. But you can stop your own frantic response to it. Be still. And in the stillness... know.

We're going to sit with this verse the way the contemplative tradition has done for centuries. Peeling it back, word by word, layer by layer. Each time, we remove a word. Each time, we go deeper. Don't rush through this. Let each version land. Let it change something in you before we move to the next.
Be still... and know that I am God.
Be still... and know that I am.
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]

What remains when all the words fall away? Just... being. Just presence. You don't have to know anything tonight. You don't have to believe anything with particular force. You don't have to understand the theology or feel the right feelings. You can simply... be. And in the being, something is known that can't be reached by thinking.

Your mind is still active. Of course it is. That's what minds do. They produce thoughts the way the heart produces beats. You can't stop it, and the attempt to stop it only creates another thought: "I can't stop thinking." So instead of fighting your thoughts, we're going to practice something gentler. We notice the thought. We give it a quiet label... just one word. And then we let it pass. Like clouds crossing a sky at night. The sky does not hold onto them. The sky does not chase them. It simply lets them move through.
When a thought comes, give it a label.
A worry about something you can't control... worrying.
A judgment about how you're doing at this very practice... judging.
A sensation in your body that pulls your attention... feeling.
An urge to move or adjust or check your phone... wanting.
An emotion that surfaces without an obvious cause... feeling.

The label doesn't have to be perfect. It's not the point. The point is the gap... that tiny space between the thought arising and you noticing it. That gap, however small, is the stillness. It's the still point the psalm is pointing to. It was there all along, underneath the noise.
Try it now. Sit in the quiet. Whatever comes, note it silently. One word. And then return to the silence. Don't follow the thought. Don't argue with it. Don't build on it. Just... label, and return.
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]

If you've been carried away by a thought... swept into a story, a plan, a worry, a memory... and then suddenly you realize it happened... that moment of realization is not failure. That moment is the practice. That is the stillness breaking through. You didn't fail. You woke up. Gently note the thought — thinking — and return to the quiet.
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]
Continue. Thoughts arise like bubbles in still water. You note them. They pop. The water is still again. And then another bubble. And you note it. And it pops. And the water is still.
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]
Good. Now let the noting go. You've practiced enough. The mind is quieter now... maybe not silent, but the spaces between thoughts are longer. The volume is lower. The urgency is gone.

In this quieter place, we're going to rest in a single word. This is centering prayer, an ancient practice from the Christian contemplative tradition. You choose one word... a word that draws you toward the presence of God... and you hold it lightly. Not gripping it. Not repeating it with effort. Just letting it rest in your awareness, the way a stone rests at the bottom of a clear lake.
Choose your word now. It might come immediately. Peace. Rest. Grace. Love. Mercy. Father. Jesus. Abide. Enough. Silence.
Whatever word arrived first... trust it. Don't second-guess it. That's the one.
Now, silently, let the word drop into the quiet. You don't have to think it loudly. Just let it be there, like a sound heard from far away. Your word.
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]

When your mind drifts... and it will, because that's what minds do... gently return to the word. Not with frustration. Not with effort. Like coming home after being out. Oh... I was somewhere else. Now I'm back. That's all. A gentle return.
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]

Let the spaces between the word grow longer. The word... then silence... then, when you need it, the word again... then longer silence. The silence is not empty. It is full. It is full of the presence you are resting in, whether you feel it or not. The feeling is not the point. The resting is.
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]

One more time. Let the word appear. And then... let even the word dissolve. Let it sink to the bottom and disappear. What remains is the silence it pointed to. The presence it was a door into. You're through the door now. You don't need the word. You are in the room.
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]
"In peace I will lie down and sleep, for you alone, Lord, make me dwell in safety." Psalm four, verse eight.

You don't need to make yourself safe tonight. You don't need to solve the unsolvable. You don't need to plan your way to peace. You don't need to control what happens while you sleep or what waits for you in the morning. You are made to dwell in safety... not by your own effort, but by the One who holds the dark hours as easily as the light. The night is His too. He does not sleep. He is not worried about tomorrow. You can borrow His peace for the next eight hours. He is offering it freely.

Lord... we have tried to be still tonight. Our minds wandered and we brought them back. They wandered again and we brought them back again. This is our offering... not perfection but presence. Imperfect, distracted, honest presence. We showed up. Meet us in the quiet... in the gaps between the thoughts... in the space where the word used to be. That's where you've always been. We know that now. Or we're beginning to. Amen.
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]

Be still. The world turns without your attention tonight. The morning will come without your effort. The people you love are held by the same God who holds you. Nothing needs you right now except the pillow and the quiet and the dark.
Just being. Just breathing. Just resting in the word... or in the silence where the word used to be.
[long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause] [long pause]

Still... quiet... safe. The thoughts thin out like stars fading at dawn, except it's the other direction... you're fading into the dark, which is where sleep lives, which is where rest has always been waiting.
The stillness deepens. The body is heavy. The breath is barely there... soft and shallow and slow.
